blocks:
Ayahs

قُلْ أَعُوذُ بِرَبِّ ٱلْفَلَقِ (١)
Say, ‘I seek the protection of the Lord of the daybreak (1)
مِن شَرِّ مَا خَلَقَ (٢)
from the evil of what He has created, (2)
وَمِن شَرِّ غَاسِقٍ إِذَا وَقَبَ (٣)
and from the evil of the dark night when it falls, (3)
وَمِن شَرِّ ٱلنَّفَّـٰثَـٰتِ فِى ٱلْعُقَدِ (٤)
and from the evil of the witches, who blow on knots, (4)
وَمِن شَرِّ حَاسِدٍ إِذَا حَسَدَ (٥)
and from the evil of the envious one when he envies.’ (5)

(Commentary)

This is a command to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to seek refuge in Allah from every evil (الشر) in general, and from certain specific evils in particular. The Surah is Medinan (مدنية), as is apparent from the reports concerning the occasion of its revelation (شأن النزول).

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ أَعُوذُ بِرَبِّ الْفَلَقِ "Say, ‘I seek out the protection of the Lord of the daybreak," Seeking refuge (العوذ) is to seek protection (الاعتصام) and to guard oneself against evil by resorting to one who can repel it. The word falaq (الفلق), with a fathah followed by a sukun, means to split or to part. With two fathahs, falaq (الفلق) is an assimilated adjective (صفة مشبهة) with the meaning of a passive participle, like qasas (القصص) meaning "that which is narrated." It is most commonly applied to the dawn (الصبح) because it is split from the darkness. Accordingly, the meaning is: "I seek refuge in the Lord of the dawn, who cleaves it and splits it." The suitability of this expression for seeking refuge from evil—which conceals good and acts as a veil before it—is manifest.

It has also been said that falaq (الفلق) refers to everything that is cleaved and split open through creation (الخلق) and origination (الإيجاد), for in creation and origination there is a splitting of non-existence (العدم) and a bringing forth of the existent into existence (الوجود); thus, it would be synonymous with "the created" (المخلوق). It has also been said that it is a pit in Hell, a view supported by some narrations.

His saying, the Exalted: مِنْ شَرِّ ما خَلَقَ "from the evil of what He has created," meaning from the evil of any being that carries evil, whether from among humans, jinn, animals, or any other created thing that possesses evil. The fact that "what He has created" (ما خلق) in its absolute sense includes evil does not necessitate that every single created thing is evil.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنْ شَرِّ غاسِقٍ إِذا وَقَبَ "from the evil of the dark night when it settles," In al-Sihah, it is stated: Ghasaq (الغسق) is the first darkness of the night; it is said "the night has darkened" (غسق الليل) when it becomes pitch black. The ghasiq (الغاسق) is the night when the twilight has disappeared. End quote. Waqub (الوقوب) means entering. Thus, the meaning is: "And from the evil of the night when it enters with its darkness." The attribution of evil to the night is because its darkness aids the evildoer in his wickedness by concealing him; thus, more evil occurs during it than during the day. Furthermore, man is weaker during the night than during the day in the face of an attacker of evil. It has also been said that ghasiq (الغاسق) refers to every attacker who assails with his evil, whoever or whatever he may be.

The mention of the evil of the night when it enters after the mention of the "evil of what He has created" is an instance of mentioning the specific after the general to emphasize its importance. In this Surah, special importance has been given to three types of evil: the evil of the night when it enters, the evil of the sorcery of magicians, and the evil of the envier when he envies, due to the prevalence of heedlessness (الغفلة) regarding them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنْ شَرِّ النَّفَّاثاتِ فِي الْعُقَدِ "from the evil of the women who blow on knots," meaning the sorceresses who perform magic by tying knots against the one being bewitched and blowing into the knots. Women were specifically mentioned because sorcery was more common among them and practiced by them more than by men. This verse contains a confirmation of the effect of sorcery (السحر) in general. Similar to it is His saying, the Exalted, in the story of Harut and Marut: فَيَتَعَلَّمُونَ مِنْهُما ما يُفَرِّقُونَ بِهِ بَيْنَ الْمَرْءِ وَزَوْجِهِ وَما هُمْ بِضارِّينَ بِهِ مِنْ أَحَدٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللهِ "From them they would learn that by which they would cause a separation between a man and his wife, though they could not harm anyone with it except by Allah’s leave" (2:102), and likewise what is found in the story of the magicians of Pharaoh.

It has also been said that "the women who blow on knots" (النفاثات في العقد) refers to women who sway the opinions of their husbands toward what they see and desire; thus, the "knot" (العقد) is the opinion (الرأي), and "blowing on the knots" is a metaphor for undoing it. However, this is a remote interpretation.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنْ شَرِّ حاسِدٍ إِذا حَسَدَ "and from the evil of the envier when he envies." meaning when he becomes occupied with envy (الحسد) and acts upon what is in his soul of envy by following through with its effects.

It has been said that the verse includes the one who casts the "evil eye" (العائن), for the eye of the one who casts it is a type of psychic envy (حسد نفساني) that manifests when he looks at something he deems great and wonders at it.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abd ibn Humayd narrated from Zayd ibn Aslam who said: "A man from the Jews bewitched the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and he fell ill. Gabriel came to him and descended with the two Surahs of Refuge (المعوذتين) and said: 'A man from the Jews has bewitched you, and the magic is in the well of so-and-so.' So he sent Ali, who brought it. He then commanded him to undo the knots and recite a verse. He began reciting and undoing until the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) stood up as if he were released from a shackle."

I say: And from the book Tibb al-A'immah, with its chain of narration to Muhammad ibn Sinan from al-Mufaddal from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام), a similar report is found. Regarding this meaning, there are many narrations from the Sunni paths with slight variations. In more than one of them, it is mentioned that he sent Zubayr and Ammar along with Ali (عليه‌السلام). There are also other narrations regarding this from the paths of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام).

As for the objection raised by some regarding the content of these narrations—that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was protected from the influence of sorcery, so how could this be? Especially since Allah the Exalted said: وَقالَ الظَّالِمُونَ إِنْ تَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا رَجُلاً مَسْحُوراً انْظُرْ كَيْفَ ضَرَبُوا لَكَ الْأَمْثالَ فَضَلُّوا فَلا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ سَبِيلاً "And the wrongdoers say, ‘You are just following a bewitched man.’ Look how they strike similitudes for you; so they have gone astray and cannot find a way" (25:8-9).

This objection is repelled by the fact that what they meant by "bewitched" (المسحور) and "mad" (المجنون) was the corruption of the intellect (العقل) through sorcery. As for his being affected by sorcery through a physical illness that afflicts his body or the like, there is no evidence for his immunity from that.

In al-Majma', it is narrated: "The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to frequently seek refuge for al-Hasan and al-Husayn (عليه‌السلام) with these two Surahs."

In the same source, from Uqbah ibn Amir who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Verses have been revealed to me the like of which have never been revealed: the two Surahs of Refuge (المعوذتان).'" This was recorded in the Sahih.

I say: It was also attributed in al-Durr al-Manthur to al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa'i, and others. Something of similar meaning was also narrated from al-Tabarani in al-Awsat from Ibn Mas'ud. Perhaps the meaning of "the like of which have never been revealed" is that they are unique in the matter of seeking refuge (العوذة), and no other Surahs share this quality with them.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ahmad, al-Bazzar, al-Tabarani, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated through authentic paths from Ibn Abbas and Ibn Mas'ud that the latter used to scratch out the two Surahs of Refuge from the Mus-haf and say: "Do not mix the Quran with what is not part of it; they are not from the Book of Allah. The Prophet was only commanded to seek refuge with them." Ibn Mas'ud would not recite them.

I say: Then al-Suyuti said: "Al-Bazzar said: 'None of the Companions followed Ibn Mas'ud in this. It has been authentically reported from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that he recited them in prayer, and they have been established in the Mus-haf.'" End quote.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, with its chain of narration to Abu Bakr al-Hadrami who said: "I said to Abu Ja'far (عليه‌السلام) that Ibn Mas'ud used to erase the two Surahs of Refuge from the Mus-haf." He replied: "My father used to say: 'Ibn Mas'ud only did that based on his own opinion, and they are both from the Quran.'"

I say: Regarding this meaning, there are many narrations from the paths of both groups (Sunni and Shi'a), in addition to the fact that there is definitive mass-transmission (التواتر القطعي) from the generality of those who profess Islam that they are part of the Quran. Some deniers of the inimitability (إعجاز) of the Quran have raised an objection that if it were inimitable in its eloquence, there would not have been a disagreement regarding these two Surahs being part of the Quran, as was the case with Ibn Mas'ud. The answer is that definitive mass-transmission is sufficient in this regard. Furthermore, no one has narrated from him that he said they were not revealed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) or that he said they were not inimitable in their eloquence; rather, he said they were not part of the Quran, and he is refuted by the mass-transmission.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir narrated from Abu Hurayrah from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) who said: "al-Falaq (الفلق) is a covered pit in Hell."

I say: There are several narrations with this meaning. In some of them, he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "A gate in the Fire which, when opened, causes Hell to blaze." This was narrated by Uqbah ibn Amir. In others: "A well in Hell which, when Hell is blazed, it is blazed from it." This was narrated by Amr ibn Anbasah, among others.

In al-Majma', it is said: "al-Falaq (الفلق) is a pit in Hell from whose intense heat the inhabitants of Hell seek refuge." This is from al-Suddi and was narrated by Abu Hamzah al-Thumali and Ali ibn Ibrahim in their commentaries.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, from his father from al-Nawfali from al-Sakuni from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Poverty is almost like disbelief (الكفر), and envy (الحسد) almost overcomes destiny (القدر).'"

I say: The narration is reported with its exact wording from Anas from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

In al-Uyun, with its chain of narration to al-Salti from Imam al-Rida from his father from his forefathers from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) who said: "Envy (الحسد) almost precedes destiny (القدر)."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Shaybah narrated from Anas who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Indeed, envy (الحسد) consumes good deeds (الحسنات) just as fire consumes wood.'"
